figures.caption: Figure 1: From CLIP/ViT to TorR. Top: dense token aligner with fixed per-frame cost. Bottom: an event-driven encoder paired with an HDC associative aligner and a lightweight reasoner. Query caching turns temporal coherence into reuse so TorR updates on change.

Figure 2: Bottleneck shift from CLIP/ViT to TorR. Left: TaskCLIP is dominated by the ViT backbone. Right: after ViT→event encoder, cost shifts to HDC associative search + graph reasoning, which are memory-bound.

Figure 4: Cache-gated HDC reasoner with 𝛿-alignment. A query cache (depth 𝐾) supplies the nearest prior query q(𝑡−1) . Partial similarity 𝜌 = cos(q(𝑡 ) , q(𝑡−1) ) = 1 − 2 |Δ | 𝐷′ selects 𝛿- update (update only flipped indices Δ) or full. Under high load, if 𝜌 ≥ 𝜏byp∧𝐻 (𝑁,𝑞), cached scores/outputs are reused. Otherwise the aligner computes 𝑠 𝑗 = cos(q, h𝑗 ) and the reasoner (for fixed task) applies cached weights ?̃? 𝑗 = cos(g𝑃 , h𝑗 ) with g𝑃 = t ⊙ 𝑟ℓ1 ⊙ · · · ⊙ 𝑟ℓ𝑘 , producing 𝑠 𝑗 = 𝑠 𝑗?̃? 𝑗 . The FPS/QoS controller gates 𝐷 ′ (bank gating).
Figure 3: TorR overview. Images are used only to transfer CLIP semantics to events. At run time, partial-similarity reuse (𝛿-updates, caches) and FPS/QoS control align cost with scene dynamics.

Figure 5: Similarity-gated top-level accelerator. PSU computes inter-query similarity 𝜌 . The FPS/QoS controller selects bypass/𝛿/full, gates 𝐷 ′ (bank enables) and precision, and programs the associative aligner. In 𝛿-mode the aligner uses a Δ-index FIFO for sparse reads from the banked item memory𝑀 × 𝐷; scores are accumulated, top-𝑘 pooled, optionally reasoned (HDC), cached, and returned via host/DMA. Solid arrows = data, dashed = control.
Table 1: Hardware specifications (TSMC 28nm, 1GHz). Logic areas in mm2, powers in mW.
